name: Stoicism
rationale: The historical Stoa and its Roman developments provide Stoicism’s primary lineage; later moral and comparative routes remain secondary.

name: Platonism
rationale: Platonism begins beside Plato and the Academy; this record’s Forum and Justice routes support thematic comparison, while separate late-antique and medieval branch records trace historical transformations.

name: Aristotelianism
rationale: Aristotle’s Lyceum setting is the defensible origin point; Islamic, Jewish, Latin, and modern-field routes represent distinct receptions.

name: Neoplatonism
rationale: Plotinus and later pagan Platonists form the tradition’s primary historical sequence; Christian and medieval transformations are later receptions.

name: Metaphysics
rationale: Reality & Being is the comparative home for the modern field, with historical lenses preventing a single tradition from owning its questions.

name: Ontology
rationale: Reality & Being provides a findable thematic home for inquiry into being, while historical routes show that ontology has no single origin.

name: Epistemology
rationale: Knowledge is the field-level home, but skeptical, South Asian, Buddhist, rationalist, and empiricist routes preserve different histories of warranted cognition.

name: Ethics
rationale: Moral Life is the broad practical-reason home; historical schools and political questions remain distinct routes rather than subfields absorbed here.

name: Virtue Ethics
rationale: The character and flourishing room best stages virtue ethics as a contested practical tradition, with ancient and modern developments linked rather than duplicated.

name: Epicureanism
rationale: The Garden and its Roman transmission provide Epicureanism’s historical home; moral-life comparisons remain secondary.

name: Cynicism
rationale: The Cynic challenge belongs in its Hellenistic practice setting, where disputed beginnings and anti-conventional performance can be explained together.

name: Skepticism
rationale: A shared historical hall can distinguish Academic and Pyrrhonian skeptical lineages while routing their later epistemological uses to the Forum.

name: Deontology
rationale: Duty, respect, consequences, and welfare is the comparative ethics home; Kant’s Enlightenment installation supplies the essential historical threshold.

name: Utilitarianism
rationale: The moral-theory room enables direct comparison of consequence and welfare, while Bentham and Mill retain their nineteenth-century primary biographies.

name: Political Philosophy
rationale: Political Action and Justice is the field-level home because authority, action, distribution, rights, and democracy require a sustained shared hall.

name: Logic
rationale: Logic & Language is the modern field home; Greek, Buddhist, Chinese, Islamic, Latin, and analytic routes prevent formal logic from appearing cultureless.

name: Philosophy of Language
rationale: Logic & Language is the findable field home, while ancient rhetoric, Buddhist semantics, Chinese naming debates, hermeneutics, and analytic work remain visible histories.
controversy_note: The modern academic field has ancient, Indian/Buddhist, Chinese, medieval, rhetorical, and interpretive prehistories.

name: Philosophy of Mind
rationale: Mind & Self is the comparative field home; South Asian, Buddhist, rationalist, phenomenological, and analytic routes show competing vocabularies and aims.
controversy_note: The human question predates the branch record’s modern start date; historical lenses must not imply a 1640 invention.

name: Philosophy of Science
rationale: Science is the comparative field home, linked to Islamic inquiry, early-modern method, empiricism, pragmatism, and analytic debates rather than a single origin story.

name: Aesthetics
rationale: Aesthetics receives a thematic Forum home, with historical routes to ancient, East Asian, Enlightenment, German Idealist, Nietzschean, and pragmatist arguments.

name: Existentialism
rationale: The postwar freedom and facticity room is the movement’s primary home; Hellenistic comparisons and nineteenth-century precursors remain explicitly non-membership routes.
controversy_note: The hall distinguishes self-identification, precursor, influence, and rejected-label statuses.

name: Phenomenology
rationale: Husserl’s method and lifeworld supply phenomenology’s historical center, with later embodiment, existence, interpretation, and critique developed around it.

name: Pragmatism
rationale: Peirce’s public, fallibilist inquiry provides the movement’s primary threshold before James, Dewey, and later continuities.

name: Analytic Philosophy
rationale: Origins in logic and analysis provide a historical threshold for diverse analytic traditions; thematic and Continental comparisons do not imply one unified doctrine.

name: Continental Philosophy
rationale: The critique hall treats Continental Philosophy as a retrospective navigation family, then routes visitors to its German, nineteenth-century, phenomenological, and critical histories.

name: Feminist Philosophy
rationale: A dedicated plural-genealogies room keeps feminist philosophy from becoming a late appendix while preserving distinct historical and liberation routes.
controversy_note: The source start year 1792 is too late for the current Mary Astell record; the hall begins with early-modern genealogies.

name: Philosophy of Religion
rationale: The Forum supplies a comparative field home, while South Asian, late-antique, Islamic, Jewish, Latin, and Enlightenment routes retain their own practices and vocabularies.

name: Ancient Greek Philosophy
rationale: This umbrella is an orientation to several Mediterranean schools, not one doctrine; routes continue into Hellenistic and late-antique transformations.

name: Chinese Philosophy
rationale: Many Ways in Early China is an umbrella orientation to competing traditions, with East Asian continuities preventing the field from ending in the Warring States.

name: Confucianism
rationale: The ritual, humaneness, cultivation, and human-nature debates form Confucianism’s classical home; later East Asian reconstructions remain a required continuation.

name: Daoism
rationale: The Daodejing and Zhuangzi room provides an attribution-aware classical home, while institutional and later East Asian developments remain a continuation route.

name: Mohism
rationale: Mohist argument receives its own bay in an early Chinese debate room; adjacency to fa/statecraft currents marks disagreement, not shared school membership.

name: Legalism
rationale: The stable search label is housed with diverse fa and statecraft currents as a retrospective grouping, not as one self-identified school.
controversy_note: Legalism is a retrospective grouping; visitor labels should foreground fa and statecraft currents.

name: Indian Philosophy
rationale: This umbrella orients visitors to many South Asian schools and acknowledged gaps; it does not turn Jain, Yoga, realist, or Vedānta traditions into one doctrine.

name: Jainism
rationale: The Jain worlds room preserves nonviolence, karma, knowledge, ascetic practice, and liberation inside a living intellectual tradition.

name: Vedanta
rationale: A rival-readings room makes Advaita, Viśiṣṭādvaita, and Dvaita commentary legible without treating any one systematizer as founder of Vedānta as a whole.

name: Buddhist Philosophy
rationale: A distinct transregional Buddhist wing preserves liberation, debate, and institutional history while routing later transformations across Asia.

name: Buddhist Epistemology
rationale: The pramāṇa lineage remains primary in Buddhist scholastic history; Knowledge and Logic are comparative lenses rather than owners of the tradition.
controversy_note: A modern umbrella label for a pramāṇa lineage; cultural-historical primary, thematic Knowledge/Logic secondary.

name: Islamic Philosophy
rationale: A dedicated Arabic and Islamic intellectual-world hall preserves original systems, debates, institutions, and post-classical continuities.
controversy_note: A distinct intellectual-world home avoids reducing Arabic/Islamic philosophy to transmission into Latin Europe.

name: Medieval Scholasticism
rationale: The Latin hall presents scholasticism as a practice and institutional culture, not as every medieval philosophy or a synonym for Islamic and Jewish thought.
controversy_note: Scholasticism is a practice and institutional culture, not a synonym for all Latin Christian philosophy.

name: Rationalism
rationale: The Cartesian foundations room provides the movement’s early-modern historical center, with later mind, metaphysics, and critical routes kept distinct.

name: Empiricism
rationale: The ideas and experience room supplies empiricism’s early-modern historical home, while science, politics, and later epistemology remain visible routes.

name: German Idealism
rationale: Post-Kantian self, activity, and freedom provides the movement’s primary threshold; Kant remains a precursor in the adjoining Enlightenment hall.
